sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I need to stop trying random permutations. Let me think about this more systematically. I've been wasting too many actions.
Key evidence so far:
1. Game over happened when placing Y at green slot 3 in the alternating arrangement (Red: O, R, N | Green: p, b, Y, ...)
2. This means Red: O, R, N and Green: p, b were correct through that point
3. The alternating arrangement Red: O, R, N | Green: p, b, M, Y didn't complete with SPACE
Wait - maybe the game over didn't mean "wrong color at this position" but rather "you placed a color that can't possibly lead to a solution." Like a constraint solver that prunes impossible branches.
But the alternating arrangement (Red: O, R, N | Green: p, b, M, Y) didn't cause game over and didn't complete either. So it's also wrong.
Let me reconsider what I know for certain:
- The palette has 7 colors: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
- Red frame has 3 slots, green frame has 4 slots
- Total = 7 slots = 7 colors
I've tried many permutations. Let me enumerate ALL possible splits and see which ones I haven't tried.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The T-connector connects the red and green frames. What if this represents a mathematical or logical operation? Like "red frame values + green frame values = top row values"?
Or what if the frames represent two groups that need to be matched to the top row in a specific way?
Let me look at the colors more carefully:
Top row (left to right): O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
What if the red frame gets colors that appear at ODD positions (1, 3, 5, 7) and green gets EVEN positions (2, 4, 6)?
Odd: O, R, N, M (4 colors)
Even: p, b, Y (3 colors)
But red has 3 slots and green has 4. So this doesn't match.
What if it's reversed? Red gets even (3), green gets odd (4)?
Red: p, b, Y
Green: O, R, N, M
I tried Red: p, b, Y | Green: O, R, N, M as "hypothesis 5" and it failed!
What about based on color brightness/warmth?
Let me try: Red frame gets the 3 "hottest" colors, green gets the 4 "coolest":
Hottest: R, O, Y
Coolest: p, b, N, M
Red: R, O, Y | Green: p, b, N, M
I think I tried this. Let me try the reverse:
Red: p, b, N | Green: R, O, Y, M
Or what about sorting by the order they appear in the rainbow?
ROYGBIV: R, O, Y, G, B, I, V
Our colors: R, O, Y, N(light green), b(blue), p(purple), M(magenta)
Rainbow order: R, O, Y, N, b, M, p (roughly)
Red (first 3): R, O, Y
Green (last 4): N, b, M, p
Hmm, I keep coming back to similar arrangements. Let me try something I'm almost certain I haven't tried:
Red: Y, O, R | Green: M, N, b, p (reverse rainbow)
Actually, let me just try the hue-sorted one I was going to try: Red: R, O, Y | Green: N, b, p, M

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try hue-sorted: Red: R, O, Y | Green: N, b, p, M
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 10},   # R
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 31},   # O
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 28},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 52},   # Y
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 24},   # N
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 45},   # b
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 28},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 17},   # p
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 38},   # M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40},
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
